comments.author: @alexd5128
comments.text: A bonus - even the French were interested in Taiwan! Taiwan was Japan's very first colony, and Japan was overwhelmed with challenges during the first few years. It changed the Taiwan governor annually for the first 3 years! The French, who had previously attempted to take Taiwan from China, made an extremely generous offer to buy Taiwan from Japan. A ranking official, Kodama, sensed that for such an offer, the French knew something valuable in Taiwan that Japan had yet to discover, and persuaded his government not to sell it by pledging his head to deliver success from Taiwan. Kodama, as a result, became Taiwan's 4th governor and worked tirelessly to turn the situation around. Though he didn't lose his head, since he succeeded, he worked himself to death!

comments.author: @alexd5128
comments.text: The sad thing is that most Westerners still attribute Taiwan's democratic reform to the KMT, when it was the obstacle.  By their simple intuition, since the KMT was** the CCP’s enemy and China is still an authoritarian state today, Taiwan’s democracy must be because of the KMT.  In reality, democracy in Taiwan means purging the KMT from power!  Many Taiwanese were martyred under the KMT dictatorship. Yes, on paper, the KMT’s late president lifted the ban on forming an opposition party and released political prisoners in 1987, but this only occurred A FEW MONTHS before his death! Was he being a reformer or an opportunist as a dying man with nothing to lose? He and his father were in charge almost continuously since 1945 with an iron fist. If the KMT was truly aspiring to democracy, they had more than 4 decades to implement it, and there shouldn’t be any political prisoner to be released shortly before his death!  **I said the KMT was the CCP’s enemy because they are no longer adversaries!  Ever since Taiwan democratized, the KMT has become addicted to losing elections.  Now, to remain relevant, the KMT has become the lackey of the CCP!  Chiang Kai-shek would turn in his grave if he knew how his party has turned out.

comments.author: @alexd5128
comments.text: Before any Chinese troll starts to inundate this board with irrelevant info like the indigenous people in Taiwan are only 2 to 3% of the population, and the majority of Taiwanese are of Chinese descent from the mainland, I would like to highlight that no modern nation defines identity based on ancestry or DNA. The Chinese view is as silly as claiming that a German American is German, a Japanese American is Japanese, and a Singaporean is Chinese. The Chinese really need to catch up with society!

comments.author: @alexd5128
comments.text: Although the Dutch East India Company wasn't interested in ruling Taiwan, they had no choice but to assume the responsibility because there was no other government to administer the island! For example, if there were a contractual dispute, who should arbitrate if a government were completely absent? Dutch missionaries even invented a written system for the native tribes so that they could conduct business with them. This wasn't like in Japan, where they could simply sign a contract with the existing government to rent a small corner in Nagasaki to conduct their trades.

comments.author: @alexd5128
comments.text: The irony is that most people in this world still think that since computer chips are mostly designed in the West, Taiwan's role in the semiconductor industry is simply doing work the West doesn't want to do by selling cheap labor. How ignorant and/or arrogant! Most consumers don't know that when it comes to computer chips, design is MUCH EASIER than building them! Chip designers are only involved with logic implementation. While I don't want to belittle logic design, no one would dispute that most countries have an army of people who know how to implement logic. For example, any engineer can easily implement a logic to perform that IF A = B, then do C, but building this circuitry as tiny as the atomic level is a completely different art! Taiwan is the only country who have figured out how to build these circuitries as small as 2nm for volume production! This means they have achieved a geometry much smaller than 2nm in their research lab, so that 2nm is mature enough for production. A virus is 100 nm for perspective. Although the production equipment is from the West, NO semiconductor manufacturers in the world follow the standard process that comes with the equipment! The easiest analogy is that I can buy the same oven used by a world-renowned chef, but I can't bake award-winning cookies because that chef has his secret recipes, whereas I follow a standard cookbook. This is why even though Samsung and Intel can buy the same ASML EUV machine, for example, they simply can't compete with Taiwan's TSMC. Can Intel or Samsung simply poach TSMC's talents? Not really. Building a chip is a 1500-step process, but each engineer is only responsible for a few steps at most. China has already tried!

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That oven analogy is genuinely one of the clearest explanations of process know-how we've seen in a comment section. Appreciate you taking the time to lay it out this thoroughly.

comments.author: @desertwanderer4283
comments.text: I love Taiwan. Spent four weeks there. I researched thoroughly where I wanted to go, read two history of Taiwan books, made my own car and hotel/BnB reservations - easy enough to do and I only know English. Used the Google translate app frequently while there. I can't adequately express how much I loved Taiwan. Amazingly interesting to walk around the cities; sometimes the narrowest alleys have unexpected shops and restaurants. Never got tired of exploring new foods at the night markets. Gorgeous mountains and northern and eastern coastlines. Stayed in Taipei, Jiufen, Taroko area, Taitung, Chishang, Mainong, Tainan, Leye village (Alishan), Dehua Settlement (Sun Moon lake), Taichung, and back to Taipei. Surprisingly well done museums of indigenous peoples and still living villages you can visit. Saw at least a couple of dozen large temples - even though there were sometimes large crowds of locals chanting and praying inside the walls, taking it very, very seriously, by the way, nobody was upset that tourists were watching. The train system in Taipei is excellent. Most of all, the atmosphere of all of Taiwan is sooo _Chill_ and unthreatening for tourists. We never felt unwelcome anywhere. We plan to go back again soon.

replies.author: @MolonLabe-1776

Wow, I’m Taiwanese American. You visited more places than most Taiwaneses do. I’m humbled to learn that how beautiful Taiwan is through videos like this and eyes of yours.
Thank you and please come back soon. We always welcome you.

comments.author: @VincentLee-pz1kw
comments.text:
Since Trump is demanding that countries pay for their national defense, perhaps the following could be considered: the Taiwanese government pays a protection fee, inviting the US to station troops in Taiwan as "mercenaries." Taiwan would provide the land and funding, while the US military would design and oversee the construction of the bases. The US military would operate all the weapons and equipment, and be responsible for the base's defense. This would allow the US military to confidently bring advanced weaponry to Taiwan, eliminating concerns about leaks and security. Taiwanese troops would be stationed around the bases, "assisting" with perimeter defense and serving as a buffer zone between the US military and local residents. This move would immediately enhance Taiwan's defense capabilities and, in a Taiwan rife with spies, fifth columnists, and pro-CCP elements, achieve the necessary secrecy and counter-espionage measures.
Or, to be even more straightforward, Trump should invite Taiwan to become a US state. If Taiwan joins the US, Trump's dream of TSMC (Taiwanese semiconductor company) would immediately become USMC (US semiconductor company). "Made in Taiwan" (MIT) would instantly become "Made in USA," and the so-called "tariff, place of origin, etc." issues between Taiwan and the US would cease to exist. At that point, the US military presence in Taiwan would be "perfectly natural." Conversely, the US economy would become Taiwan's economic hinterland, solving the problem of Taiwan's small market. Taiwanese people and supply chains could choose to relocate to the US mainland, while the US military would actively develop Taiwan into an "unsinkable aircraft carrier in the first island chain." If the CCP insists on using force against Taiwan, it would be tantamount to "declaring war on the US." I believe that given the CCP's cowardly and bullying nature, the US military's power is sufficient to deter any CCP action against Taiwan and prevent World War III!

comments.author: @WXYZ-968
comments.text: >>>>>  At time point 20:15 -- historically and legally, Taiwan was NOT "handed (from Japan) to Republic of China," after WW2, despite prior stated intentions to do so (per 1943 Cairo Declaration and 1945 Potsdam Proclamation), as the legal instruments to officially carry out that intention (per 1951 Treaty of San Francisco and 1952 Treaty of Taipei) did NOT ultimately include that stipulation, thus resulting in Taiwan's current official status as being "undetermined," despite having been a thriving freedom- and democracy-based de facto independent country for around 80+ years!  <<<<<

replies.author: @kenc7249
replies.text: Thank you for your post!

replies.author: @WXYZ-968
replies.text:
@alexd5128 -- correct ... with the following clarifications, if I may.
I actually prefer to no longer use the term "China," since (Imperial) China stopped existing once Republic of China (RoC) was formed in 1912 to replace the Qing Dynasty. After 1949, PRC was formed as the original "renegade" from RoC, despite RoC's retreating to Taiwan. So now there are PRC on the mainland and RoC on Taiwan, with the original (Imperial) China no longer in existence. But many will still use "China" as a colloquial shorthand reference to PRC, just as "Taiwan" is a colloquial shorthand reference to RoC; however, note that there are no official PRC government documents that refer solely to "China" when referencing itself (eg, look at their passports and what it displays on its cover). 

We tend to think that the PRC vs. RoC civil war of long ago had pragmatically "ended" in 1949, but PRC does NOT hold that view and continues to bluster about its "primacy" over RoC, since no formal treaty has officially "ended" that civil war.

This next tidbit becomes rather contentious, but I think that actual history and international law should still prevail -- after WW2, USA was given jurisdictional authority to handle dispositions of post-WW2 territorial settlement matters, which meant that, during that period of time, USA had full jurisdiction over the territories of Taiwan and affiliated islands (with shortened reference as just Taiwan). And, since neither Treaties of SF or Taipei resolved the final disposition of Taiwan (thus its currently stated status as "undetermined"), it has been claimed by some experts that Taiwan's actual current status is actually still under that left-over WW2 jurisdictional authority of USA! Which means that the current RoC government on Taiwan can be said to "reside" there as a "guest" of USA, hence conferring its status as a "de facto" (but NOT "de jure") independent country! 

Think about the implication of what this situation means, in terms of who actually still (today) holds jurisdictional authority over Taiwan -- is Taiwan factually and legally still (today) a "territory" of USA, from those post-WW2 times? Just like Guam and a bunch of other scattered USA island territories across the south Pacific? And what does that, then, mean, for USA to be "obligated" to defend Taiwan, as a jurisdictional USA territory, against annexation by PRC?  ???

replies.author: @alexd5128
replies.text: Totally agreed!  Chinese trolls like to argue that China doesn't recognize the SF Peace Treaty. However, any sensible person would immediately realize why a Chinese signature is even needed. If Paul acquires a property from John and later decides to get rid of it, does Paul have to consult with John at all? For those who are curious, the SF Peace Treaty didn't transfer Taiwan to China but only made Japan relinquish Taiwan unilaterally because the Chinese Civil War ended in 1949, but the SF treaty wasn't signed until 1951. Thus, rather than taking a position on the subject, the allies chose to kick the can down the road instead.

replies.author: @nisstw
replies.text:
​@qweszxcqazesc 明明是地方政府該做的事，又推給中央
2014年黑心油事件，馬英九政府立法院獨大，將食安責任督察責任改為地方政府，在2014年11月18日 由立法院三讀通過《食品安全衛生管理法》修正案。

修法後，各縣市直轄市政府（衛生局）負責事項包括：第一線現地查核、市場抽驗、受理民眾檢舉，以及對違規業者執行罰鍰、下架沒入與停業裁罰。
＿＿＿
條文重點大意分別為
《食安法》第 41 條第一項。直轄市、縣（市）主管機關為確保食品符合本法規定，得進入製造、加工、調配、包裝、運送、貯存、販賣場所執行現地查核、抽樣檢驗，並得命暫停作業、封存產品。

《食安法》第 43 條第一項及第二項。
主管機關對於檢舉查獲違反本法規定之食品或食品業者，除應對檢舉人身分資料嚴守秘密外，並得酌予獎勵。其檢舉、受理、獎勵辦法則依據此條文授權訂定。

《食安法》第 44 條第一項 與 第 47 條。
當業者違反衛生安全規定時（例如販售過期、非法添加物食品），主管機關可處新臺幣 6 萬元以上 2 億元以下罰鍰；情節重大者，並「得命其歇業、停業一定期間、廢止其公司、商業、工廠之全部或部分登記事項」。

台灣中央政府並未包庇製油商，而是來自中國政黨的地方政府掩蓋真相，這個中國政黨，長期得到製油商的金錢資助。
＿＿＿＿＿
根據監察院政治獻金公開查閱平台的資料，中聯油脂現任董事、福壽實業董事長洪堯昆歷年來的個人政治獻金，清一色均捐贈給國民黨籍的政治人物與候選人。
2024年總統大選：曾捐款給國民黨提名的候選人侯友宜。
2024年立委選舉：曾捐款給多位國民黨立委（如鄭天財、馬文君、萬美玲、洪孟楷等）。
沒有捐給任何民進黨。
包括下游自檢未依規定第一時間通報，現也被連帶罰款的南僑，政治現金都是給泛藍，幾乎都是國民黨。
地方首長：新北市長侯友宜（獲捐 50 萬元）。
立法委員：王鴻薇（獲捐 40 萬元，為民代中最多）、牛煦庭、林德福、羅明才、李彥秀等。
地方議員：秦慧珠、游淑慧、鍾小平、郭昭嚴、侯漢廷（新黨）等台北市議員。

親 中國國民 黨的企業，長期受到包庇，才是國家亂源！

comments.author: @seanyang3
comments.text: Thank you for the remarkable storytelling of an extraordinary land. As a Taiwanese living overseas for 30 years, I can say this captures the essence of Taiwan's history.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Coming from someone who's carried Taiwan with them for thirty years overseas, that means a great deal to hear. Thank you for watching and for saying so.

comments.author: @EugeneU-au1
comments.text: Thank you. I think you have presented quite well the essense of the island and its people and what makes Taiwan what it is today. A people happy to serve the world but just wants to be left in peace. A unique democracy in a communist Chinese sea with its own culture and unique attributes. A people that remembers its past but would like to chart its own future.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thank you — that's exactly the balance we were hoping would come through: proud of where it's been, clear-eyed about wanting to shape where it's going.
